title: A resolution calling on the United Nations Security Council to enforce the existing arms embargo on Darfur and extend it to cover all of Sudan.
plain_summary: This resolution is about the violence and killing happening in Sudan, a country in Africa. Two military groups have been fighting each other since April 2023, which has hurt and killed many people. The US Senate wants the United Nations to stop all countries from selling weapons to Sudan, not just to one part called Darfur like they do now. The Senate also wants the US government to do more to help people in Sudan get food and medical care, and to keep track of the violence happening there.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations. (text: CR S1716-1717)

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This is a resolution calling on the United Nations Security Council to enforce the existing arms embargo on Darfur and extend it to cover all of Sudan.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: The Senate recognizes that a violent conflict started on April 15, 2023, between two military groups in Sudan. These groups are the Rapid Support Forces led by Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo and the Sudanese Armed Forces led by Abdel Fattah al-Burhan. This fighting has killed tens of thousands of civilians and forced over twelve and a half million people from their homes. The violence happening now is similar to the genocide that occurred in Sudan's Darfur region in the early 2000s. In 2004, the United Nations Security Council created rules to stop weapons from being sold to non-government groups in Darfur. These rules were expanded in 2005 to include all fighters in Darfur, including the Sudanese government. The rules were made stronger in 2010 and renewed in 2024. However, countries from the Middle East, Africa, Asia, and Europe are still providing weapons to both military groups. Reports show that weapons from many different countries have arrived in Sudan between 2004 and 2023. These weapons include small arms, drones, explosives, and other military equipment from multiple foreign countries. The weapons are being used both inside and outside of Darfur in areas controlled by either military group. The conflict has destroyed cities across Sudan, including the capital Khartoum. Both military groups have committed mass atrocities in these cities. In February 2025, the Rapid Support Forces attacked a camp for displaced people in Darfur, killing humanitarian workers and committing other violent acts. The United States Secretary of State has determined that both groups have committed war crimes, and that the Rapid Support Forces has committed genocide, crimes against humanity, and ethnic cleansing. The conflict has caused a cholera outbreak with over 550,000 cases and more than 1,500 deaths. Women and children have been subjected to torture and sexual violence. More than 220 cases of child rape have been reported since early 2024. Both military groups are forcing children to become soldiers. Despite these problems, only one person has ever been punished for violating the weapons ban in Darfur.

sections.title: What the Senate Calls For
sections.text: The Senate condemns the violent acts committed by all fighting groups in Sudan. The Senate condemns the genocide by the Rapid Support Forces and allied militias against the Masalit people and other non-Arab ethnic groups in Darfur. The Senate calls for an immediate end to the war and all violence in Sudan. The Senate calls on the United Nations Security Council to expand the Darfur arms embargo to apply to all of Sudan, include dual-use equipment in the list of banned materials, create stricter enforcement to hold violators accountable, and establish ways to deliver humanitarian aid and protect civilians. The Senate calls on the United Nations General Assembly to pass a resolution calling for a nationwide ceasefire, recognizing the atrocities in Sudan, and calling for a more effective arms embargo, unfettered humanitarian aid delivery, and civilian protection. The Senate calls on the United States Government to increase support for local organizations monitoring atrocities and weapons deliveries, improve mechanisms for monitoring weapons supply chains, resume funding for foreign assistance to famine-stricken areas of Sudan, develop support systems for victims of sexual violence, and work with the United Nations, African Union, and other partners to condemn atrocities, support an arms embargo, ensure humanitarian aid delivery, protect civilians, pressure the fighting groups to end the conflict, and pressure external actors to follow the arms embargo.
